name: Sparta
alt: Welcome to Sparta, N.C. stone sign
tagline: The quiet heart of Alleghany County — Christmas-tree farms, the New River, and our home office on US-21.
paragraphs: Sparta is the county seat of Alleghany County and the town Greenfield Acres Realty calls home. Set just off the Blue Ridge Parkway near Milepost 229, it pairs a walkable Main Street — galleries, the Alleghany Jubilee dance hall, Muddy Creek Café, Laconia Ale Works — with some of the most affordable mountain land in the High Country.

Buyers here find rolling Christmas-tree farms, long-range pasture views, and frontage on the ancient New River. It's the kind of town where the unofficial motto is 'what happens in Sparta gets home before you do' — and where your broker is also your neighbor.

name: Blowing Rock
alt: Welcome to Blowing Rock sign with spring tulips
tagline: The crown of the Blue Ridge Parkway — storybook village charm with the High Country's finest estates.
paragraphs: Blowing Rock is the storybook village of the Blue Ridge — a Main Street of boutiques, inns, and restaurants that has drawn visitors for more than a century, wrapped by the carriage trails of Moses Cone and Julian Price parks.

It is also the High Country's premier luxury market. Gated golf communities, historic summer estates, and long-range-view homes here command the region's top prices, and marketing them well is exactly what Aimee's CLHMS GUILD designation is for.

name: Boone
alt: Autumn color over King Street in downtown Boone
tagline: The hub of the High Country — college-town energy, four-season trails, and strong investment demand.
paragraphs: Boone is the beating heart of the High Country: home to Appalachian State University, a lively King Street of shops and restaurants, and the region's hospitals, schools, and services.
That mix keeps demand strong year-round — from primary homes and mountain retreats to some of the strongest short-term-rental and investment fundamentals in western North Carolina.

name: Banner Elk
alt: Welcome to Banner Elk sign with autumn pumpkins and mums
tagline: The ski capital of the South — a 3,700-foot valley town ringed by gated mountain communities.
paragraphs: Banner Elk sits in the center of the High Country at 3,700 feet — the ski capital of the South, tucked between Sugar Mountain and Beech Mountain resorts. It's the destination for thousands who love the outdoors and southern mountain culture.

Many of the region's finest gated communities — Diamond Creek, Eagles Nest, The Lodges at Eagles Nest among them — are nestled in Banner Elk's countryside, making it a cornerstone of the High Country luxury market.

name: Beech Mountain
alt: Welcome to Beech Mountain sign at the resort village
tagline: Eastern America's highest town — ski-in living at 5,506 feet.

facts.knownFor: Highest town in the East · Ski resort

paragraphs: At 5,506 feet, Beech Mountain is the highest incorporated town in eastern America — a four-season resort community where summer temperatures rarely leave the 70s and winter delivers real, reliable snow.
Homes here range from classic A-frame ski chalets to view estates above the clouds. For buyers wanting cool summers, ski access, and rental potential in one address, Beech is hard to beat.

name: Jefferson
alt: Sunbeams and rhododendron on the ridge above Jefferson
tagline: Ashe County's seat, in the shadow of Mount Jefferson.
paragraphs: Jefferson is the county seat of Ashe County, sitting literally in the shadow of Mount Jefferson State Natural Area — 4,683 feet of protected summit trails rising straight from town.
It offers some of the High Country's best value: established neighborhoods, farmland, and view lots within minutes of West Jefferson's arts district and the New River.

name: West Jefferson
alt: Shops along the main street of downtown West Jefferson
tagline: The High Country's arts town — murals, galleries, and the famous frescoes.
paragraphs: West Jefferson has transformed into one of North Carolina's great small arts towns — a walkable downtown of murals, galleries, and shops anchored by Ashe County Cheese and the famed churches of the frescoes.
Its blend of culture and countryside draws retirees and second-home buyers alike: in-town cottages, mountain-view homes, and Christmas-tree-farm acreage all trade within a few minutes' drive.

name: Lansing
alt: Aerial view of the village of Lansing among green hills
tagline: A tiny rail town reborn — creekside quiet in far-north Ashe County.
paragraphs: Lansing is the High Country at its quietest — a historic railroad village in northern Ashe County where Big Horse Creek runs past a handful of shops, a community park, and genuinely friendly porches.
Buyers come here for creek frontage, small farms, and privacy that's getting hard to find, all within striking distance of West Jefferson and the Virginia line.

name: Laurel Springs
alt: Nursery rows at a Laurel Springs tree farm
tagline: Parkway crossroads country — Doughton Park's 30 miles of trails out your back door.
paragraphs: Laurel Springs sits where the Blue Ridge Parkway meets NC-18, making it a favorite of buyers who want the Parkway as their daily drive. Doughton Park — 30 miles of trails, trout streams, and preserved homesteads — is effectively the neighborhood park.
Cabins, cattle farms, and long-view acreage define the market here, and several of Aimee's happiest clients (see the reviews) found their mountain home in Laurel Springs.

name: Glade Valley
alt: Green bald mountain rising over Glade Valley
tagline: Rolling farm valley between Sparta and Roaring Gap.
paragraphs: Glade Valley rolls out between Sparta and Roaring Gap in a patchwork of pastures, ponds, and woodlots under the Parkway's ridge line — classic Alleghany farm country.
It's a place people fall for on a Sunday drive and never leave: ranch homes with Blue Ridge backdrops, small farms, and quiet gravel-road privacy minutes from town.

name: Piney Creek
alt: Autumn farm fields rolling toward the mountains at Piney Creek
tagline: New River frontage on the Virginia line.
paragraphs: Piney Creek hugs the New River in Alleghany County's northwest corner, right on the Virginia line — prime territory for anglers, paddlers, and anyone who wants one of the world's oldest rivers in the front yard.
River-frontage homes and farms here are treasured and don't change hands often; when they do, they move quickly. Tell us what you're after and we'll watch the market for you.

name: Roaring Gap
alt: Mountain lake in the Roaring Gap resort community
tagline: A historic resort enclave — golf, lakes, and gated estates on the escarpment.
paragraphs: Roaring Gap has been a refuge for discerning families since the 1890s, perched on the Blue Ridge escarpment where the mountains fall away toward the Piedmont. Gated communities like Olde Beau — with its celebrated mountain golf course — and High Meadows define the market.
Estates here pair country-club amenities with staggering escarpment views and an easy hour's drive from Winston-Salem, making it a natural fit for Triad buyers seeking a legacy mountain home.

name: Crumpler
alt: Kayaks on the New River at Crumpler
tagline: New River farm country in northern Ashe County.
paragraphs: Crumpler is New River farm country — a scattering of pastures, stone farmhouses, and river bottoms along the North Fork in northern Ashe County, where outfitters like Dusty Trails put paddlers on the water all summer.
The market here is farms, acreage, and river-view homesteads: properties with real land and real character, often at prices that surprise buyers from busier mountain towns.
